card.indications: Treatment of seizure disorders and panic disorder. Uses and dosing depend on diagnosis, age, response, and concurrent therapy.
card.mechanism: Enhances gamma-aminobutyric acid activity at GABA-A receptors, increasing inhibitory signaling in the central nervous system.
card.dosing: Begin with a low dose and increase gradually. Panic-disorder dosing commonly starts at 0.25 mg twice daily; seizure dosing is individualized and divided. Older adults and patients with hepatic impairment generally require conservative dosing.
card.routeAdministration: Give on a consistent schedule. Orally disintegrating tablets should be handled with dry hands and placed on the tongue. Do not stop abruptly after regular use because withdrawal and seizures may occur.
card.contraindications: Significant liver disease, acute narrow-angle glaucoma, or hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines. Avoid combining with opioids unless no safer alternative exists.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings address profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death with opioids; abuse, misuse, addiction, physical dependence, and withdrawal. May impair coordination and cognition and increase falls.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, dizziness, fatigue, impaired coordination, memory difficulty, depression, and increased salivation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Respiratory depression, severe sedation, suicidal thoughts or behavior, paradoxical agitation, dependence, withdrawal seizures, and serious allergic reactions.
card.interactions: Opioids, alcohol, sleep medications, antihistamines, antipsychotics, and other CNS depressants increase sedation and respiratory risk. CYP3A inhibitors may increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Assess seizure frequency or panic symptoms, respiratory status, sedation, gait, fall risk, mood, suicidal thinking, misuse risk, and signs of tolerance or withdrawal.
card.patientTeaching: Take only as prescribed, avoid alcohol, and do not drive until effects are known. Never share the medication. Do not stop suddenly. Seek emergency help for slowed breathing, extreme sleepiness, or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol. Maintain adequate hydration and regular meals if nausea or appetite changes occur.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension; extended-release products are also used for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. Other uses depend on formulation and prescriber direction.
card.mechanism: Stimulates central alpha2 receptors, decreasing sympathetic outflow and lowering heart rate, vascular resistance, and blood pressure.
card.dosing: Hypertension dosing is individualized and often divided twice daily. Extended-release ADHD products use a separate titration schedule and are not interchangeable milligram for milligram with immediate-release tablets.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure and pulse before administration according to parameters. Swallow extended-release tablets whole. Apply patches to clean, dry, hairless skin and rotate sites. Taper gradually rather than stopping abruptly.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clonidine or patch components. Use caution with bradycardia, conduction disease, cerebrovascular disease, renal impairment, and severe coronary disease.
card.warnings: Abrupt withdrawal can cause rebound hypertension, agitation, headache, tremor, and rarely stroke or death. Can cause marked sedation, bradycardia, hypotension, and conduction abnormalities.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, drowsiness, dizziness, constipation, fatigue, headache, and local patch irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe rebound hypertension, syncope, marked bradycardia, heart block, severe hypotension, and serious allergic skin reactions to the patch.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives increase hypotension. Beta blockers and rate-slowing drugs may worsen bradycardia. CNS depressants increase sedation. Tricyclic antidepressants may reduce the antihypertensive effect.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, orthostatic symptoms, sedation, adherence, patch site, and signs of rebound hypertension. Monitor more closely during dose changes.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as scheduled and never stop suddenly. Rise slowly. Avoid alcohol and driving until effects are known. Replace patches on schedule, remove the old patch, and fold it adhesive sides together for disposal.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration and fiber for dry mouth and constipation. Follow prescribed sodium guidance for hypertension and avoid alcohol because it increases sedation.

card.indications: Reduction of atherothrombotic events in patients with recent myocardial infarction, recent ischemic stroke, established peripheral arterial disease, or acute coronary syndrome, often with aspirin depending on indication.
card.mechanism: Its active metabolite irreversibly blocks platelet P2Y12 ADP receptors, preventing activation of the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa complex and reducing platelet aggregation for the life of the platelet.
card.dosing: Common maintenance dosing is 75 mg once daily. Acute coronary syndrome may require a loading dose followed by daily therapy according to the treatment plan.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily with or without food. Do not stop before surgery, stent therapy, or other procedures unless the prescribing clinician directs it because premature interruption can cause thrombosis.
card.contraindications: Active pathologic bleeding such as peptic-ulcer or intracranial bleeding, or serious hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause major or fatal bleeding. Effectiveness may be reduced in CYP2C19 poor metabolizers or with strong CYP2C19 inhibitors. Rare thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura can occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Bruising, nosebleeds, gastrointestinal upset, diarrhea, rash, and minor bleeding.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Major gastrointestinal or intracranial hemorrhage, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, severe neutropenia, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: NSAIDs, aspirin, anticoagulants, SSRIs, SNRIs, and other antiplatelet agents increase bleeding risk. Omeprazole and esomeprazole may reduce antiplatelet activation through CYP2C19 inhibition.
card.monitoring: Assess for bleeding, bruising, melena, hematuria, neurologic changes, CBC when indicated, adherence, and planned procedures. Evaluate unexplained fever, weakness, renal changes, or thrombocytopenia for possible TTP.
card.patientTeaching: Take every day and do not stop without prescriber approval. Report black stools, vomiting blood, persistent nosebleeds, unusual bruising, severe headache, weakness, or speech changes. Inform dentists and surgeons before procedures.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid excessive alcohol because it increases bleeding risk. Do not add aspirin, NSAIDs, or herbal products such as ginkgo without approval.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible superficial fungal infections such as tinea, cutaneous candidiasis, vulvovaginal candidiasis, and oral candidiasis depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Inhibits fungal ergosterol synthesis by blocking a cytochrome P450-dependent enzyme, disrupting the fungal cell membrane.
card.dosing: Topical products are commonly applied twice daily for the labeled treatment duration. Vaginal and oral-troche regimens vary by product and diagnosis. Complete the full course even if symptoms improve.
card.routeAdministration: Clean and dry the affected area before topical application. Apply a thin layer and wash hands afterward. Troches should dissolve slowly in the mouth and should not be chewed or swallowed whole. Vaginal products are for intravaginal use only.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to clotrimazole or product ingredients. Oral troches require caution in liver disease.
card.warnings: Avoid eye exposure. Stop and seek guidance for severe irritation, swelling, rash, or lack of improvement. Oral troches can rarely cause abnormal liver tests.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Local burning, stinging, itching, redness, irritation, abdominal discomfort with troches, and altered taste.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypersensitivity, significant local swelling or blistering, and clinically important hepatic injury with oral troches.
card.interactions: Topical use has few systemic interactions. Oral clotrimazole may affect drugs metabolized through CYP3A pathways. Vaginal products may weaken latex condoms or diaphragms depending on formulation.
card.monitoring: Assess lesion appearance, itching, drainage, symptom improvement, secondary infection, local irritation, and recurrence. Monitor liver function when prolonged oral-troche therapy is used in patients at risk.
card.patientTeaching: Use for the entire prescribed or labeled duration. Keep skin areas clean and dry. Do not share towels or applicators. Seek care for fever, pelvic pain, foul discharge, recurrent infection, or symptoms that do not improve.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions for topical or vaginal products. Allow oral troches to dissolve after meals when prescribed and avoid eating or drinking immediately afterward if directed.

card.indications: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia and reduction of recurrent suicidal behavior in schizophrenia or schizoaffective disorder.
card.mechanism: Antagonizes multiple dopamine and serotonin receptors and also has adrenergic, histamine, and muscarinic effects.
card.dosing: Start at 12.5 mg once or twice daily and titrate gradually to reduce hypotension, bradycardia, syncope, and seizure risk. Treatment interruptions may require restarting at a low dose.
card.routeAdministration: Verify required absolute neutrophil count monitoring and current prescribing requirements. Give consistently with or without food. Shake suspension well. Do not resume the prior full dose after an interruption without prescriber guidance.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity. Use caution or avoid in severe neutropenia, uncontrolled epilepsy, severe cardiac disease, paralytic ileus, and conditions that increase aspiration or constipation risk.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include severe neutropenia, orthostatic hypotension/bradycardia/syncope, seizures, myocarditis/cardiomyopathy, and increased mortality in older adults with dementia-related psychosis. Severe gastrointestinal hypomotility can be fatal.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Sedation, dizziness, tachycardia, hypersalivation, constipation, weight gain, fever, headache, and orthostatic hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Agranulocytosis or severe neutropenia, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, seizures, severe constipation or ileus, aspiration pneumonia, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, metabolic complications, and fatal arrhythmias.
card.interactions: Smoking changes and CYP1A2 inhibitors or inducers can markedly alter levels. CNS depressants increase sedation. Anticholinergics increase constipation and urinary retention. Drugs that suppress bone marrow or prolong QT require caution.
card.monitoring: Monitor ANC according to current requirements, temperature and infection symptoms, bowel function, blood pressure, pulse, weight, glucose, lipids, myocarditis symptoms, ECG when indicated, seizures, swallowing, and adherence.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever, sore throat, infection, chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting, seizures, or reduced bowel movements immediately. Do not stop or restart independently. Avoid alcohol, rise slowly, and tell the prescriber about smoking changes.
card.nutrition: Maintain fluids and fiber unless contraindicated because constipation can become life-threatening. Monitor weight and metabolic health. Keep caffeine and smoking habits consistent because changes can alter clozapine levels.
